notes: Positions computed dynamically via WASM ephemeris. This registry provides physical metadata only. Sources: NASA Planetary Fact Sheets, JPL SSD, IAU 2015 nominal values.

bodies.name: Sun
notable_features: Corona visible during eclipses
11-year sunspot cycle
Solar wind extending to heliopause (~120 AU)
bodies.description: A G2V main-sequence star containing 99.86% of the Solar System's mass. Its core fuses 600 million tonnes of hydrogen per second at 15.7 million K.

bodies.name: Mercury
notable_features: Caloris Basin (1,550 km impact crater)
3:2 spin-orbit resonance
Extreme temperature range (-180 to 430 C)
Large iron core (~85% of radius)
bodies.description: The smallest and innermost planet, with a heavily cratered surface resembling Earth's Moon. Its oversized iron core generates a weak but detectable magnetic field.

bodies.name: Venus
notable_features: Retrograde rotation (spins opposite to orbit)
Runaway greenhouse effect (462 C surface)
Sulfuric acid cloud layers at 45-70 km
Maxwell Montes (highest point, 11 km)
bodies.description: Earth's near-twin in size, shrouded in opaque sulfuric acid clouds. Its extreme greenhouse effect makes it the hottest planet despite being farther from the Sun than Mercury.

bodies.name: Earth
notable_features: 71% surface covered by liquid water oceans
Active plate tectonics
Strong magnetic field from liquid iron outer core
Only known body with life
bodies.description: The densest planet in the Solar System and the only one known to harbor life. Its liquid water oceans, active geology, and protective magnetic field create a uniquely habitable environment.

bodies.name: Mars
notable_features: Olympus Mons (tallest volcano, 21.9 km)
Valles Marineris (4,000 km canyon system)
Polar ice caps (water and CO2 ice)
Evidence of ancient liquid water
bodies.description: The Red Planet, colored by iron oxide dust. Its thin atmosphere and frozen surface belie a dynamic geological past with massive volcanoes and canyon systems dwarfing anything on Earth.

bodies.name: Jupiter

rings.description: Faint ring system of dust particles, discovered by Voyager 1 in 1979.

notable_features: Great Red Spot (persistent anticyclonic storm, ~16,000 km wide)
Strongest magnetic field of any planet (20,000x Earth)
95 known moons including four Galilean moons
Internal heat source (radiates 1.67x received solar energy)
bodies.description: The largest planet, containing more mass than all other planets combined. A gas giant with no solid surface, its banded clouds and Great Red Spot are driven by internal heat and rapid rotation.

bodies.name: Saturn

rings.description: Spectacular ring system of ice and rock particles, from dust-sized to house-sized, with gaps including the Cassini Division.

notable_features: Extensive ring system (mostly water ice, <100 million years old)
Hexagonal polar vortex at north pole
Lowest density of any planet (0.687 g/cm3, would float on water)
146 known moons
bodies.description: The jewel of the Solar System, distinguished by its spectacular ring system. A gas giant with density lower than water, its rapid rotation creates a visibly oblate shape.

bodies.name: Uranus

rings.description: Narrow, dark ring system of centimeter to meter-sized particles.

notable_features: Extreme axial tilt (97.77 degrees, rolls on its side)
Methane atmosphere gives blue-green color
Coldest planetary atmosphere (-224 C minimum)
28 known moons named after Shakespeare and Pope characters
bodies.description: An ice giant tilted on its side, likely by an ancient collision. Its featureless blue-green appearance belies a dynamic interior with a mantle of water, methane, and ammonia ices.

bodies.name: Neptune

rings.description: Five faint rings of dust and ice particles, with clumpy arcs in the Adams ring.

notable_features: Fastest winds in the Solar System (2,100 km/h)
Deep blue color from methane absorption
Strong internal heat source (2.6x received solar energy)
16 known moons including captured Triton
bodies.description: The outermost planet, a deep blue ice giant with the strongest sustained winds of any planet. Discovered in 1846 through mathematical prediction before visual confirmation.

bodies.name: Pluto
notable_features: Heart-shaped Tombaugh Regio (nitrogen ice plain)
Binary system with Charon (1/8 of Pluto's mass)
Highly eccentric orbit crosses Neptune's
Five known moons
bodies.description: A dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt, revealed by New Horizons (2015) as a geologically complex world with nitrogen glaciers, water ice mountains, and a thin haze-layered atmosphere.

bodies.name: Ceres
notable_features: Largest object in the asteroid belt
Bright spots in Occator Crater (sodium carbonate deposits)
Subsurface brine reservoir
Cryovolcanic activity (Ahuna Mons)
bodies.description: The largest body in the asteroid belt and the smallest recognized dwarf planet. Dawn spacecraft (2015-2018) revealed bright salt deposits and evidence of a subsurface ocean.

bodies.name: Eris
notable_features: Most massive known dwarf planet
Discovery triggered Pluto's reclassification (2006)
One known moon: Dysnomia
Highly inclined orbit (44 degrees)
bodies.description: The most massive known dwarf planet, orbiting in the scattered disc far beyond Neptune. Its 2005 discovery directly led to the IAU redefining 'planet' and Pluto's reclassification.

bodies.name: Haumea

rings.description: Narrow ring ~70 km wide, discovered via stellar occultation in 2017.

notable_features: Fastest rotation of any known equilibrium body (3.9 hours)
Elongated ellipsoidal shape from rapid spin
Has a ring system (rare for small bodies)
Two moons: Hi'iaka and Namaka
bodies.description: A rapidly spinning dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt with an elongated ellipsoidal shape caused by its ~4-hour rotation period — the fastest of any known body in hydrostatic equilibrium.

bodies.name: Makemake
notable_features: Second-brightest Kuiper belt object after Pluto
Surface of frozen methane, ethane, and nitrogen
One known moon: S/2015 (136472) 1 (MK2)
Extremely cold surface (~40 K)
bodies.description: A classical Kuiper belt dwarf planet with a reddish-brown surface coated in frozen methane and ethane. One of the largest known trans-Neptunian objects.

bodies.name: Moon
notable_features: Tidally locked (same face always toward Earth)
Maria (dark basaltic plains from ancient volcanism)
South Pole-Aitken Basin (largest known impact structure, 2,500 km)
Stabilizes Earth's axial tilt
bodies.description: Earth's only natural satellite, formed ~4.5 billion years ago likely from a giant impact. The fifth-largest moon in the Solar System, its gravitational influence drives Earth's tides.

bodies.name: Io
notable_features: Most volcanically active body in the Solar System
Over 400 active volcanoes
Sulfur-coated surface gives yellow-orange color
Tidal heating from Jupiter and Europa/Ganymede resonance
bodies.description: Jupiter's innermost Galilean moon and the most volcanically active body known. Tidal forces from Jupiter flex its interior, driving over 400 active volcanoes that constantly resurface it.

bodies.name: Europa
notable_features: Global subsurface ocean beneath ice shell
Smoothest surface in the Solar System
Lineae (crisscrossing fracture patterns)
Prime candidate for extraterrestrial life
bodies.description: A Galilean moon encased in a smooth ice shell hiding a global salt-water ocean containing twice the water of Earth's oceans. A top target for astrobiology missions.

bodies.name: Ganymede
notable_features: Largest moon in the Solar System (larger than Mercury)
Only moon with its own magnetic field
Mixed terrain of old cratered and young grooved regions
Subsurface ocean sandwiched between ice layers
bodies.description: The largest moon in the Solar System, bigger than Mercury. The only moon known to generate its own magnetic field, likely from convection in its iron core.

bodies.name: Callisto
notable_features: Most heavily cratered body in the Solar System
Valhalla multi-ring impact structure (3,800 km across)
Possible subsurface ocean
Not in orbital resonance with other Galilean moons
bodies.description: The outermost Galilean moon, with the oldest and most heavily cratered surface in the Solar System. Orbits beyond Jupiter's main radiation belts, making it a candidate for a future human base.

bodies.name: Titan
notable_features: Only moon with a dense atmosphere (1.47 atm surface pressure)
Methane/ethane rain, rivers, and hydrocarbon lakes (Kraken Mare)
Organic-rich haze gives orange appearance
Huygens probe landed 2005-01-14
bodies.description: Saturn's largest moon and the only moon with a substantial atmosphere. Its nitrogen-methane atmosphere supports a methane cycle analogous to Earth's water cycle, with rain, rivers, and seas.

bodies.name: Triton
notable_features: Retrograde orbit (only large moon with this property)
Likely a captured Kuiper belt object
Active nitrogen geysers observed by Voyager 2
Cantaloupe terrain (unique dimpled surface)
bodies.description: Neptune's largest moon, orbiting retrograde — almost certainly a captured Kuiper belt object. Voyager 2 observed active nitrogen geysers erupting from its frozen surface.

bodies.name: Phobos
notable_features: Stickney crater (9.7 km, nearly shattered the moon)
Orbiting below synchronous altitude (will crash into Mars in ~50 Myr)
Extremely low density suggests rubble pile interior
Orbits Mars 3 times per Martian day
bodies.description: Mars's larger moon, an irregularly shaped body only 27 km across. It orbits so close to Mars that it rises in the west and sets in the east, completing three orbits per Martian day.

bodies.name: Deimos
notable_features: Smooth surface (filled-in craters from regolith)
Slowly spiraling outward from Mars
One of the smallest known moons
bodies.description: Mars's smaller, outer moon. Only 15 km across with a relatively smooth surface where regolith has filled in impact craters. Slowly spiraling away from Mars.

bodies.name: Enceladus
notable_features: Cryovolcanic water geysers at south pole (tiger stripes)
Global subsurface salt-water ocean
Feeds Saturn's E ring with ejected ice particles
Hydrothermal vents on ocean floor (potential habitability)
bodies.description: A tiny moon with enormous astrobiological significance. Cassini detected salt-water geysers erupting from its south pole, fed by a global subsurface ocean with hydrothermal activity.

bodies.name: Miranda
notable_features: Verona Rupes (20 km cliff, tallest known in Solar System)
Coronae (large oval features with concentric ridges)
Extreme surface variation suggests violent geological history
May have been shattered and reassembled
bodies.description: Uranus's smallest major moon, with a bizarre patchwork surface featuring the tallest known cliff in the Solar System (Verona Rupes, ~20 km). May have been shattered by impacts and gravitationally reassembled.

bodies.name: Charon
notable_features: Mutually tidally locked with Pluto (face each other perpetually)
Mass ratio to Pluto (1:8) makes it nearly a binary system
Mordor Macula (dark reddish polar cap from Pluto's escaped atmosphere)
Vast canyon system (Serenity Chasma)
bodies.description: Pluto's largest moon, so massive relative to Pluto that they orbit a common center of gravity above Pluto's surface — effectively a binary system. Revealed by New Horizons as a geologically diverse world.

bodies.name: 1P/Halley
notable_features: Most famous periodic comet, recorded since 240 BC
Retrograde orbit (orbits opposite to planets)
Parent body of Eta Aquariid and Orionid meteor showers
Visited by Giotto spacecraft in 1986
bodies.description: The most famous periodic comet, visible from Earth every 75–79 years. Last perihelion was February 1986; next expected around July 2061. Its retrograde orbit and high eccentricity make it a dramatic visitor to the inner solar system.

bodies.name: Hale-Bopp
notable_features: Great Comet of 1997 — visible to naked eye for 18 months
Unusually large nucleus (~30 km radius)
Three distinct tails (dust, sodium, ion)
Orbit nearly perpendicular to ecliptic plane
bodies.description: One of the most widely observed comets in history, Hale-Bopp was visible to the naked eye for a record 18 months in 1996–1997. Its enormous nucleus and long orbital period (~2,520 years) place it among the great long-period comets.

bodies.name: C/2020 F3 (NEOWISE)
notable_features: Brightest comet in the Northern Hemisphere since Hale-Bopp
Discovered by NEOWISE space telescope in March 2020
Perihelion passage July 3, 2020 at 0.29 AU from Sun
Prominent dust and ion tails
bodies.description: The brightest comet visible from the Northern Hemisphere since Hale-Bopp. Discovered in March 2020 by the NEOWISE infrared space telescope, it provided a spectacular naked-eye display in July 2020. Its orbital period of ~6,800 years means it won't return until roughly 8800 AD.

bodies.name: 2P/Encke
notable_features: Shortest known orbital period of any comet (3.3 years)
Parent body of the Taurid meteor stream
Nearly exhausted volatile ices — faint compared to historical brightness
Second comet ever to have its orbit predicted (by Johann Franz Encke)
bodies.description: The comet with the shortest known orbital period at just 3.3 years. Parent body of the Taurid meteor stream, Encke has been slowly losing its volatile ices and is now relatively faint, but remains an important object for studying comet evolution.

bodies.name: 55P/Tempel-Tuttle
notable_features: Parent body of the Leonid meteor shower
Retrograde orbit with 162° inclination
Produces dramatic Leonid meteor storms every ~33 years
Last perihelion: 1998, next: ~2031
bodies.description: A periodic comet and the parent body of the spectacular Leonid meteor shower. Every ~33 years when Earth passes through fresh debris near the comet's path, the Leonids can produce dramatic meteor storms with thousands of meteors per hour.

bodies.name: 109P/Swift-Tuttle
notable_features: Parent body of the Perseid meteor shower
One of the largest known periodic comet nuclei (~26 km diameter)
Retrograde orbit with 113° inclination
Has been called 'the single most dangerous object known to humanity' due to near-miss potential
bodies.description: A large periodic comet and parent body of the Perseid meteor shower, one of the most popular annual meteor displays. With a nucleus diameter of ~26 km, it is one of the largest objects known to make repeated close approaches to Earth's orbit.

bodies.name: 4 Vesta
notable_features: Second-largest asteroid by mass
Giant south pole impact basin (Rheasilvia, 505 km)
Source of HED meteorites found on Earth
Differentiated interior with iron core
bodies.description: The second-largest body in the asteroid belt, explored by NASA's Dawn spacecraft (2011-2012). Its differentiated interior and massive south-pole impact basin make it a unique remnant of the early Solar System.

bodies.name: 2 Pallas
notable_features: Third-largest asteroid by volume
Exceptionally high orbital inclination (34.8 degrees)
Heavily cratered surface
Extreme axial tilt (~84 degrees)
bodies.description: The third-largest asteroid, notable for its high orbital inclination (34.8 degrees) which makes it difficult to visit with spacecraft. Its extreme axial tilt makes it one of the most tilted bodies in the Solar System.

bodies.name: 10 Hygiea
notable_features: Fourth-largest asteroid
Nearly spherical — candidate dwarf planet
Namesake of the Hygiea asteroid family
Dark C-type (carbonaceous) surface
bodies.description: The fourth-largest body in the asteroid belt and the largest member of the Hygiea family. VLT/SPHERE imaging revealed it is nearly spherical, making it a candidate for dwarf planet reclassification.

bodies.name: 50000 Quaoar
notable_features: Classical Kuiper belt object at 43.7 AU
Ring system discovered in 2023 (unexpectedly far from body)
Moon Weywot in orbit
Crystalline water ice and ammonia hydrate on surface
bodies.description: A classical Kuiper belt object roughly half the size of Pluto. In 2023, stellar occultations revealed it has a ring system at an unexpectedly large distance, challenging theories of ring confinement.

bodies.name: 90377 Sedna
notable_features: Extremely distant orbit: perihelion 76 AU, aphelion ~937 AU
Orbital period ~11,400 years
One of the reddest objects in the Solar System
Possible evidence for Planet Nine or stellar encounter
bodies.description: A detached trans-Neptunian object with one of the most distant known orbits. Its extreme orbit (a ~507 AU) cannot be explained by known planet perturbations, making it key evidence in the search for Planet Nine.

bodies.name: 90482 Orcus
notable_features: 3:2 resonance with Neptune (Plutino)
Called 'anti-Pluto' — similar orbit but opposite orbital phase
Large moon Vanth (~443 km)
Water ice and possibly ammonia on surface
bodies.description: A Plutino in 3:2 mean-motion resonance with Neptune, sometimes called the 'anti-Pluto' because its orbital position is roughly opposite to Pluto's. Its large moon Vanth is about half Orcus's diameter.

satellites.name: International Space Station
satellites.description: The largest modular space station in low Earth orbit, continuously occupied since November 2000. A joint project of NASA, Roscosmos, JAXA, ESA, and CSA.

satellites.name: James Webb Space Telescope
satellites.description: The most powerful space telescope ever built, observing in infrared from the Sun-Earth L2 point. Successor to Hubble with a 6.5m gold-coated beryllium primary mirror.

satellites.name: Hubble Space Telescope
satellites.description: A UV/visible/near-IR space telescope that revolutionized observational astronomy. Serviced five times by Space Shuttle crews, it has been operational for over 35 years.

satellites.name: Chandra X-ray Observatory
satellites.description: NASA's flagship X-ray telescope in a highly elliptical orbit, providing unprecedented angular resolution of X-ray sources. Named after astrophysicist Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar.

satellites.name: Voyager 1
satellites.description: The most distant human-made object, now in interstellar space beyond the heliopause since August 2012. Carries the Golden Record, a message to any extraterrestrial intelligence.

satellites.name: Voyager 2
satellites.description: The only spacecraft to visit all four giant planets. Now in interstellar space since November 2018, heading in a different direction from Voyager 1.

satellites.name: Pioneer 10
satellites.description: First spacecraft to traverse the asteroid belt and fly by Jupiter. Contact lost in January 2003 at ~80 AU. Carries the Pioneer plaque depicting humans and Earth's location.

satellites.name: Pioneer 11
satellites.description: First spacecraft to fly by Saturn, providing the first close-up images of its rings. Contact lost in November 1995. Heading toward the constellation Aquila.

satellites.name: New Horizons
satellites.description: First spacecraft to fly by Pluto (2015) and a Kuiper belt object (Arrokoth, 2019). Now heading out of the Solar System, continuing to study the outer heliosphere.

satellites.name: Starlink (constellation)
satellites.description: SpaceX's satellite internet constellation of ~6,000+ operational satellites in low Earth orbit, providing global broadband coverage. Mass per satellite listed.

satellites.name: GPS (constellation)
satellites.description: The US Global Positioning System constellation of ~31 active satellites in medium Earth orbit, providing global navigation and timing services. Mass is approximate per satellite.

satellites.name: Sputnik 1
satellites.description: The first artificial Earth satellite, launched by the Soviet Union. Its radio beacons transmitted for 21 days, and it burned up on re-entry on January 4, 1958.

satellites.name: Apollo 11 Landing Site
satellites.description: Tranquility Base — site of the first crewed Moon landing on July 20, 1969. Astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin spent 21.5 hours on the surface.

satellites.name: Apollo 17 Landing Site
satellites.description: Taurus-Littrow valley — site of the last crewed Moon landing (December 11, 1972) and the longest crewed lunar mission. Geologist Harrison Schmitt collected orange soil indicating volcanic activity.
